stem: [V3-MAPPED-2026] The nurse is caring for a 58-year-old male who presented to the emergency department with crushing chest pain. Review the client's electronic health record. Complete the bowtie by identifying the most likely condition, two priority nursing actions, and two parameters to monitor.

tabs.title: Patient Information
tabs.content: Name: John Smith Age: 58 years Gender: Male Code Status: Full Code Allergies: No Known Allergies Past Medical History: Hypertension Social History: Smoker (1 pack/day for 20 years) ISO: Standard Precautions

tabs.title: SBAR
tabs.content: Situation: At 09:30, this is the nurse for John Smith, a 58-year-old male in ED 4, presenting with acute, crushing chest pain rated 10/10. Background: He has a history of hypertension and is a smoker. The pain started 30 minutes ago while shoveling snow and radiates to his jaw. Assessment: The client is anxious and diaphoretic. Vitals: BP 164/98 mmHg, HR 108/min, RR 24/min, SpO2 92% on room air. A 12-lead EKG shows ST-elevation in leads V2-V4. Recommendation: The client is experiencing an acute anterior STEMI. I have initiated the STEMI protocol. Requesting immediate cardiology consultation and cath lab activation.

tabs.title: Vital Signs
tabs.content: Time: 09:30 Blood Pressure: 164/98 mmHg Heart Rate: 108/min Respiratory Rate: 24/min SpO2: 92% on room air Pain: 10/10, crushing, substernal, radiates to jaw

tabs.title: Diagnostic Results
tabs.content: 12-Lead EKG Interpretation: Sinus Tachycardia with 2mm ST-segment elevation in leads V2, V3, V4. Consistent with acute anterior wall myocardial infarction. Cardiac Biomarkers: - Troponin I: 0.08 ng/mL (Reference: <0.04 ng/mL) - CK-MB: 4.2 ng/mL (Reference: 0-5.0 ng/mL)

tabs.title: Physical Exam
tabs.content: General: Anxious, diaphoretic male in acute distress. Cardiovascular: Tachycardia, S4 heart sound present, no murmurs auscultated. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally. Respiratory: Increased work of breathing, lung sounds clear bilaterally. Neurological: Alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Complains of jaw pain. Integumentary: Skin pale, cool, and clammy.

tabs.title: Radiology
tabs.content: Chest X-Ray: No acute cardiopulmonary abnormalities identified. Heart size appears within normal limits. No evidence of pulmonary edema or pneumothorax.

tabs.title: Care Plan
tabs.content: Priority Nursing Diagnoses: - Acute Pain related to myocardial ischemia as evidenced by patient report of 10/10 chest pain and elevated vital signs. - Decreased Cardiac Output related to altered contractility secondary to myocardial infarction as evidenced by EKG changes and elevated cardiac biomarkers. - Anxiety related to acute health event as evidenced by patient report of feeling anxious and increased respiratory rate. Nursing Interventions: - Administer oxygen to maintain SpO2 >95%. - Administer prescribed medications (aspirin, nitroglycerin, morphine) as ordered. - Continuous cardiac monitoring for arrhythmias and ST-segment changes. - Provide emotional support and reduce anxiety through clear communication and calm demeanor. - Prepare patient for emergent cardiac catheterization.

tabs.title: MAR
tabs.content: Aspirin 324 mg PO, Chewable, STAT Nitroglycerin 0.4 mg SL, PRN for chest pain, repeat every 5 minutes x 3 doses Morphine Sulfate 2-4 mg IV, PRN for pain, titrate to effect Oxygen 2L via Nasal Cannula, Continuous, titrate to maintain SpO2 >95%

hoverRationales.c1: This is incorrect because stable angina pain is typically relieved by rest and does not cause ST-elevation or troponin release. The client's persistent, severe pain and EKG changes indicate acute infarction.
hoverRationales.c2: ST-elevation in V2-V4 indicates transmural ischemia of the anterior myocardial wall, typically due to occlusion of the left anterior descending artery. The client's symptoms and elevated troponin confirm acute myocardial infarction.
hoverRationales.c3: This is incorrect as costochondritis is chest wall inflammation, which would not cause EKG changes like ST-elevation or elevated cardiac biomarkers. The client's presentation is classic for a cardiac, not musculoskeletal, emergency.
hoverRationales.a1: Aspirin inhibits cyclooxygenase-1 (COX-1) within platelets, preventing the formation of thromboxane A2. This action reduces platelet aggregation and thrombus formation at the site of coronary artery plaque rupture, limiting infarct size.
hoverRationales.a2: Emergent cardiac catheterization for percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) is the gold standard for reperfusion in STEMI. It mechanically opens the occluded artery, restoring blood flow to the ischemic myocardium and preserving heart muscle.
hoverRationales.a3: This action is contraindicated and dangerous. Ambulation increases myocardial oxygen demand, which would worsen ischemia and potentially extend the size of the infarction in a client experiencing a STEMI.
hoverRationales.a4: This is incorrect because NSAIDs (except aspirin) are contraindicated in acute MI. They can increase the risk of mortality, reinfarction, and heart failure by impairing healing and promoting adverse ventricular remodeling.
hoverRationales.p1: Continuous ST-segment monitoring provides real-time data on myocardial ischemia. It allows for immediate detection of re-occlusion or worsening ischemia, guiding timely intervention and assessing the effectiveness of reperfusion therapy.
hoverRationales.p2: Troponin is a protein released from necrotic cardiomyocytes. Serial measurements quantify the extent of myocardial injury, help determine the peak of the infarction, and can detect reinfarction if levels rise again after an initial decline.
hoverRationales.p3: This parameter is irrelevant to the acute management of a STEMI. It is typically monitored for conditions like ascites or bowel obstruction, not acute coronary syndrome.
hoverRationales.p4: This parameter is used to monitor respiratory conditions like asthma. While the client has an increased respiratory rate, it is due to pain and anxiety from the cardiac event, not an underlying obstructive airway disease.

pearls.title: Early Recognition of STEMI
pearls.description: Prompt identification of STEMI through EKG and symptom assessment is crucial for timely intervention and improved patient outcomes.
pearls.strategy: Always consider cardiac etiology in patients presenting with chest pain, especially with associated symptoms like diaphoresis and jaw pain. Obtain a 12-lead EKG immediately.

pearls.title: Importance of Aspirin
pearls.description: Aspirin is a cornerstone of STEMI treatment, inhibiting platelet aggregation and reducing thrombus formation.
pearls.strategy: Administer aspirin as soon as possible upon suspicion of STEMI, unless contraindicated. Ensure the patient chews the aspirin for faster absorption.

traps.title: Delaying Reperfusion Therapy
traps.description: Any delay in initiating reperfusion therapy (PCI) can lead to increased myocardial damage and poorer outcomes.
traps.strategy: Activating the STEMI protocol immediately and expediting transfer to the cardiac catheterization lab are essential. Minimize any delays in the process.

traps.title: Administering NSAIDs
traps.description: NSAIDs (except aspirin) are contraindicated in STEMI due to increased risk of adverse cardiovascular events.
traps.strategy: Avoid administering NSAIDs for pain relief in STEMI patients. Use alternative analgesics like morphine, as prescribed.

mnemonics.title: MONA
mnemonics.description: Mnemonic for initial STEMI management: Morphine, Oxygen, Nitroglycerin, Aspirin
mnemonics.strategy: Remember the MONA mnemonic to guide initial interventions in STEMI patients. Prioritize aspirin administration and oxygen supplementation.

mnemonics.title: TIME
mnemonics.description: Mnemonic for STEMI treatment goals: Time is muscle.
mnemonics.strategy: Emphasize the importance of rapid treatment to preserve heart muscle. Every minute counts in STEMI management.
